topic.name: The Boat Race History
topic.description: A historical overview of the annual rowing competition between the Universities of Oxford and Cambridge.

The race is a side-by-side rowing race between crews from the Universities of Oxford and Cambridge along the River Thames.

The race was first held in 1829, and takes place on the River Thames in southwest London.

The race was first held in 1829, and since 1845 has taken place on the 4.2-mile Championship Course on the River Thames.
Document 7: The 28th Boat Race between crews from the University of Oxford and the University of Cambridge took place on the River Thames on 1 April 1871.

Document 8: The 16th Boat Race took place on 15 April 1859.

topic.name: Naval Warships of the 20th Century
topic.description: This topic encompasses the design, construction, and operational history of various classes of naval warships primarily from the early to mid-20th century, focusing on their roles in significant historical conflicts.

Document 1: The Stalingrad-class battlecruiser was a Soviet Union battlecruiser design from 1941.
The design would have been able to outgun any ship with similar speed, or outrun anything more heavily armed.
Document 2: The Renown class comprised a pair of battlecruisers built during the First World War for the Royal Navy.
They were the world 's largest and fastest capital ships upon completion.
Document 3: The E and F-class destroyers were a group of 18 destroyers built for the Royal Navy during the 1930s.
Most of the sisters were committed to the Norwegian Campaign in April – June where they helped to sink one German destroyer and a submarine.
Document 4: The Majestic class of nine pre-dreadnought battleships were built for the Royal Navy in the mid-1890s.
The ships proved to be among the most successful designs of their day.
Document 5: The Borodino-class battleship was a class of five pre-dreadnought battleships built for the Imperial Russian Navy around the end of the 19th century.
They were among the ships ordered to sail from the Baltic Sea to the Far East to relieve the Pacific Squadron besieged by the Japanese in Port Arthur.
Document 6: The Lion class were a class of battlecruisers built for the British Royal Navy before World War I.
These improvements were in response to the German Moltke class, the first German battlecruisers.
Document 7: The G- and H-class destroyers were a group of 18 destroyers built for the Royal Navy during the 1930s.
They began to escort convoys and patrol for German submarines and commerce raiders.
Document 8: The Chacal-class destroyer were a group of six large destroyers built for the French Navy during the 1920s.
Their primary role was scouting for the battleline.
Document 9: Asama was the lead ship of her class of armored cruisers built for the Imperial Japanese Navy in the late 1890s.
She served in the Russo-Japanese War of 1904 – 05.
Document 10: Admiral Scheer was a Deutschland-class heavy cruiser which served with the Kriegsmarine of Nazi Germany during World War II.
The ship was nominally under the 10,000 long tons limitation on warship size imposed by the Treaty of Versailles.

topic.name: Historical Figures in Politics and Charity
topic.description: This cluster discusses the lives and contributions of various historical figures who have played significant roles in politics, charity, and social movements.

1: Venerable Ho was affectionately known as Singapore's 'grand dame of charity' in recognition of her lifelong devotion in helping the old and needy.

2: Leonid Brezhnev was the leader of the CPSU from 1964 until his death in 1982, whose eighteen-year reign was recognised as the time of social and economic stagnation in the late Soviet Union.

3: Ira Roe Foster was a teacher, medical doctor, attorney, soldier, businessman, and politician.

5: Alfonsina Orsini was a Regent of Florence who governed the Republic of Florence during the absences of her son.

6: John Van Antwerp MacMurray was an American attorney, author, and diplomat best known as one of the leading China experts in the U.S. government.

7: Jon Gundersen Hol was a Norwegian engineer and activist known for his pamphlet that called for soldiers and civilians to arm themselves during a politically unstable time.

9: Moses Gill was a Massachusetts politician who briefly served as the state's Acting Governor.

10: Thomas Henry Hines was a Confederate cavalryman known for his spying activities during the American Civil War.

topic.name: Hurricane and Cyclone Seasons
topic.description: The documents cover various hurricane and cyclone seasons, detailing their formation, impacts, and historical significance.

Document 1: Hurricane Inez was a major hurricane that affected the Caribbean, Bahamas, Florida, and Mexico in 1966.
Damage on Guadeloupe was estimated at $50 million, and there were 40 deaths.
Document 2: The 1969 Atlantic hurricane season was the busiest Atlantic hurricane season since 1933.
The most significant storm of the season was Hurricane Camille, which peaked as a Category 5 hurricane.
Document 3: Hurricane Bonnie was a major hurricane that made landfall in North Carolina, United States, inflicting severe crop damage.
Fearing a major hurricane strike, coastal locations from Florida to Virginia performed extensive preparations.
Document 4: The 1989–90 South-West Indian Ocean cyclone season was an average cyclone season, with nine named storms and five tropical cyclones.
Cyclone Alibera was the second longest-lasting tropical cyclone on record in the basin.
Document 5: The 1928 Atlantic hurricane season featured the Okeechobee hurricane, which was the second deadliest tropical cyclone in the history of the United States.
The most significant storm of the season was Hurricane Four, nicknamed the Okeechobee hurricane.
Document 6: The 1998–99 South-West Indian Ocean cyclone season was a quiet season that had the fourth-lowest number of days with tropical storm or tropical cyclone activity.
There were only two tropical cyclones – a storm with winds of at least 120 km/h.
Document 7: The 1994 Pacific typhoon season was an extremely active season in the annual cycle of tropical cyclone formation in the Western North Pacific.
The strongest storm of the season was Super Typhoon Doug.
Document 8: The 1926 Atlantic hurricane season featured the highest number of major hurricanes at the time.
At least eleven tropical cyclones developed during the season.
Document 9: The 2003 North Indian Ocean cyclone season was mostly focused in the Bay of Bengal, where six of the seven depressions developed.

topic.name: Medieval Political Conflicts

2: The Arab–Khazar wars were a series of conflicts fought between the armies of the Khazar Khaganate and the Umayyad Caliphate.

3: The southward expansion of the Han dynasty comprises a series of military campaigns.
Military expansion to the south began under the previous Qin dynasty and continued during the Han.

5: Edward I was involved early in the political intrigues of his father's reign.

6: Justin was an East Roman (Byzantine) aristocrat and general.

7: The Greco-Persian Wars were a series of conflicts between the Achaemenid Empire of Persia and Greek city-states.

8: Amenemhat IV was the seventh and penultimate pharaoh of the 12th Dynasty of Egypt.

9: The Livonian War was fought for control of Old Livonia when the Tsardom of Russia faced off against a varying coalition.
Russia dominated the region with early military successes.
10: Theodore Komnenos Doukas was ruler of Epirus and Thessaly from 1215 to 1230.

topic.name: World War II Military Units

6: The 27th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army... During World War II the battalion was used mainly in a garrison role until the last year of the war.

topic.name: Fungal Species and Taxonomy
topic.description: The documents discuss various species of fungi, their characteristics, taxonomy, and ecological roles.

Document 1: Leotia lubrica, commonly referred to as a jelly baby, is a species of fungus in the family Leotiaceae.
The species feeds as a saprotroph.
Document 2: Protomycena is an extinct monotypic genus of gilled fungus in the family Mycenaceae.
The genus is known only from the holotype specimen.
Document 3: Mycena leptocephala, commonly known as the nitrous bonnet, is a species of fungus in the Mycenaceae family.
The mushroom is found in North America and Asia.
Document 4: Leccinum manzanitae is an edible species of bolete fungus in the family Boletaceae.
It is commonly known as the manzanita bolete.
Document 5: Volvariella surrecta, commonly known as the piggyback rosegill, is an agaric fungus in the family Pluteaceae.
The fungus grows as a parasite on the fruit bodies of other gilled mushrooms.
Document 6: Pseudoplectania nigrella, commonly known as the ebony cup, is a species of fungi in the family Sarcosomataceae.
The fungus produces a unique chemical compound, plectasin.
Document 7: Amanita ceciliae, commonly called snakeskin grisette, is a basidiomycete fungus in the genus Amanita.
The mushrooms are considered edible, but field guides typically advise caution.
Document 8: Podoserpula is a genus of fungi in the family Amylocorticiaceae.
The genus contains two species: the type, P. pusio, commonly known as the pagoda fungus.
Document 9: Tricholoma vaccinum, commonly known as the russet scaly tricholoma, is a fungus of the agaric genus Tricholoma.
The fungus grows in a mycorrhizal association with spruce or pine trees.
Document 10: Lysurus mokusin, commonly known as the lantern stinkhorn, is a saprobic species of fungus in the family Phallaceae.
The fruit body consists of a reddish, cylindrical fluted stipe.

2: Marrakesh is a major city of the Kingdom of Morocco
Located to the north of the foothills of the snow-capped Atlas Mountains
3: Wales is bordered by England to its east
It has a total area of 2,064,100 hectares
5: The Shumen Plateau Nature Park is located in the Shumen Plateau
the park encloses the Bukaka Reserve Forest
6: Raasay is an island between the Isle of Skye and the mainland of Scotland
Its terrain is varied
8: The River Weaver is a river, navigable in its lower reaches
Improvements to the river to make it navigable were authorised in 1720
9: Bedford Castle was a large medieval castle in Bedford, England
The castle played a prominent part in both the civil war of the Anarchy
10: The Alconétar Bridge was a Roman segmental arch bridge in the Extremadura region, Spain
The ancient structure is one of the earliest of its kind

topic.name: Tropical Cyclones in the Atlantic and Pacific
topic.description: The documents discuss various tropical cyclones, including their formation, paths, intensities, impacts, and historical significance in both the Atlantic and Pacific regions.

Document 1: Tropical Storm Colin was a tropical cyclone that brought squally weather to Bermuda and caused extensive rip currents across the East Coast of the United States in August 2010.
Colin developed as a tropical depression from a low-pressure area in the central Atlantic on August 2.
Document 2: Tropical Storm Gabrielle caused moderate flooding in northeastern Mexico and southern Texas in August 1995.
Gabrielle developed from a tropical wave in the west-central Gulf of Mexico on August 9.
Document 4: Hurricane Norbert marked the first time a core of a hurricane was fully mapped in three-dimensions.
Norbert gradually intensified, reaching hurricane intensity two days after formation.
Document 5: Tropical Storm Arlene, the first named storm of the 2011 Atlantic hurricane season, brought blustery conditions to much of eastern Mexico.
Arlene originated from an Atlantic tropical wave, which crossed the Yucatán Peninsula before emerging over warm waters in the Bay of Campeche.
Document 8: Tropical Storm Trudy was a short-lived tropical cyclone in October 2014 that caused significant flooding in southern Mexico.
The storm originated from an area of low pressure associated with a monsoon trough near Central America.
